title: Squeezed families hit the brakes on spending
full_text:
Rising interest rates and fluctuating fuel costs has slowed household spending at the same time as businesses offered massive discounts to attract customers.
Commonwealth Bank figures based on payments data of seven million Australians shows spending ticked up just 0.3 per cent in June.
Commonwealth Bank said the most noted slowdown in its household spending index was household goods.
This was despite retailers offering heavy discounts as they looked to boost sales and clear excess stock at the end of the financial year.
Commonwealth Bank head of Australian economics Belinda Allen said higher-for-longer interest rates, inflation and the Middle East conflict had put the brakes on consumer spending.
“The last three months have seen some volatile moves in the household spending index due to the up and down of petrol prices, seasonality around payments of bills for education and utilities, as well the timing of sales,” she said.
According to the Commonwealth Bank, June’s figures fit in with a broader spending slowdown, with monthly increases at just 0.3 per cent for the first six months of the year.
Spending on hospitality rose only marginally by 0.1 per cent in June, compared with 0.9 per cent growth in May, indicating that sporting events hosted through June did little to boost spending growth.
Ms Allen said while the June inflation figures were yet to be released, by May the CPI rose by 4 per cent for the year, meaning the 0.3 per cent jump in spending could just be due to rising costs over an increase in volume.
Ms Allen also warns of more pain to come for Australian businesses, as households continue to cut back on spending.
“Slower household income growth together with the ‘wealth effect’ from a downturn in the housing market is expected to weigh on spending,” she said.
In the lead-up to the end of the financial year, households were hit with two major blows – an increase in interest rates and rapidly changing fuel prices.
In May, the RBA lifted interest rates by 25 basis points for the third meeting in a row, taking the cash rate to 4.35 per cent.
By May 15, all four of the major banks had passed on the interest rate pain to variable rate holders, while Macquarie lifted its rates on May 22.
Canstar.com.au analysis shows the RBA’s increase will add about $91 to the monthly repayments of a $600,000 mortgage with 25 years remaining.
Across all three hikes, the extra payments total $272 a month. If the cash rate remains steady for the next 12 months, that’s an extra $3265 over the next year.
While mortgage holders were harder hit due to rising repayments, motorists had a mixed experience at the petrol pump.
Higher costs due to the US and Iran war were in part offset by the government’s halving of the fuel excise that lowered costs by 32c per litre.
The Commonwealth Bank said these higher interest rates and the petrol prices led to a rapid deceleration in recreational spending, which slumped from 2.3 per cent in May to just 0.2 per cent growth in June.
Lower spending on ski resorts, camping stores, museums and galleries and tour operators sapped spending momentum in the category, although this was in part due to poor weather at the start of the ski season. Offsetting these falls were “solid” gains in annual spending on online travel bookings, commercial airlines, fitness clubs and gyms, travel agencies and sporting goods stores, helping keep the category in the green.
